utterances: The Senate opened debate today on the bipartisan immigration reform plan backed by President Bush.
Opponents immediately vowed to change it.
But supporters said amendments could threaten the delicately negotiated measure even before it gets to the House.
In a minute, we'll talk to freshmen House members about their views.
First, in the Senate, the immigration bill drew a steady stream of objections from the left and the right.
I would ask why it is that we don't take out those things that are not good.
That's Alabama's Republican Senator Jeff Sessions.
He says there is much to strike from the plan.
Like jumping across a 10-foot cabin, jumping nine feet is not good enough.
You jump nine feet, you still fall to your doom.
North Dakota Senator Byron Dorgan, a Democrat, spoke against the provision to bring in some 400,000 temporary guest workers each year.
The issue of temporary workers is an important one because we live at a time in this country where there is downward pressure on income for American families.

language.
McCune was famous inside the prison system.
He had survived death row when he had written a song called ""My Last Mile"" about his experiences facing electric chair.
(Soundbite of archived interview)
This song was composed on the death row in 1951 by Billy McCune.
(Singing) When they put those straps on me, I know I must go.
They can kill my body but cannot kill my soul.
He had formed a good friendship with the warden, Warden Sublet.

It was Sublet who drove through the night on the rainy night just before the execution and reached the governor of Texas and was able to persuade him to adjudicate his sentence and change it to life in prison.
(Soundbite of archived interview)
(Singing) When they put that current to me, I know I can't smile but I want that Lord with me as I walk my last mile.
How's that?
Yeah, that was good.
Do you have an advice?
Do you have an opinion?
That was good.
The recording was the only one I made in the Texas prison.
And in the process of it, he started to tell me his whole life story.
(Soundbite of archived interview)
Here I am, my name is Billy McCune.
My prison number is 12-20-54.
I was sentenced to die…
Once the tape recorder was on, it was like he was on the radio and was addressing a huge audience like it was a live broadcast from inside his cell.
(Soundbite of archived interview)
People have often asked, what is a prison cell like?
There is a door that is opened by a crank up in front.
I guess there was so much I wanted to tell about the prison.
(Soundbite of archived interview)
I am entirely alone and I have always been alone.
Suddenly, Billy's words were a way to express the horror of this place much better than I could.
(Soundbite of archived interview)
I receive no Christmas cards from any of my relatives.
No letters, no brothers, no sisters, no mothers, no dads.
I receive no visit in the last 16 years.
So you out there, please, take my warning and try to change your way.
Out there, you can do many things - dance, skate.
You will not be thrown into a bed of rattlesnakes.
This is just one guy speaking to us - one voice.
There's a little God in everybody, and that's what you're listening to.
(Soundbite of archived interview)
Any man confined to a cell learns to do various things, as I have done.
He has learned to read.
He sings to his self and try to rehabilitate ourself, if such a thing is possible.
He knew the Bic refill to do any writing, so I got him those and I got him some paper and some art materials.
The result was this just tremendous amount of artwork came out of this guy.
There was like a flame from hell.
(Soundbite of archived interview)
He tries to write a little poetry and poems.
At one point, he said to me, you know, I wrote my life story, but I threw it out, and I was just crushed.
And I looked at him.
He said, well, I can just write it again.
And he wrote it again.
(Soundbite of archived interview)
I hope this brief little story that I have told you will give you an idea of a convict or inmate and how he lives in prison.
Thank you very much.
I would originally just had the run of the place, really.
As time went on, they started withdrawing the hospitality mat.
So I had to go to Billy and say goodbye.
(Soundbite of archived interview)
I'm pretty sure that I will never again go out of this prison walls alive.
But I will have faith and I will have hope in the goodness and the humanism of the human race.
Goodbye and may God bless you.
My name is Billy McCune.
(Soundbite of music)
So, I left Texas, I went back to New York.
I finished doing the book and it was published - ""Conversations with the Dead"" - they never showed a copy to McCune.
And then, two years after that, I was able to get Billy's book published.
That was called ""The Autobiography of Billy McCune.
And then about five years after that, they let him go.
About 20 years went by, I got a letter in the mail and he was living in a city in the Midwest and he wanted to hear from me.
So I went out to see him.
(Soundbite of archived interview)
Well, it's good to see you.
Yeah.
